text: Atom transmutation—selective core skeletal modification—constitutes an important aspect of small-molecule drug development and molecular imaging. Herein, we show that a range of nitrogen-containing heteroaromatics, including both bioactive pyrimidines and pyridines, can be successfully subjected to $^{14}$N to $^{15}$N atom transmutation in a quantitative, isotropic fashion under mild conditions. Our skeletal editing approach initially involves $N$-triflylation to enhance the reactivity of the aromatic ring, followed by a zwitterionic room temperature Zincke-type ring-opening/ring-closing reaction with an $^{15}$N-enriched aspartate. This reaction affords an $N$-succinyl derivative that is stoichiometrically converted to the $^{15}$N-labeled heterocycle upon base catalysed neutralization. Our protocol is shown to be applicable to a wide variety of both functionalised and bioactive heteroaromatics. An optimised downstream work-up is also demonstrated to deliver product of enhanced $^{15}$N-isotopic purity equal to that of the $^{15}$N aspartate reactant. Using a combination of density functional theory and decision tree analysis we ascertain a subset of stereo-electronic variables that serve to reliably assess the potential for $^{14}$N to $^{15}$N exchange across different hetero-cyclic systems. Our $^{14}$N to $^{15}$N isotopic exchange protocol also affords products that are amenable for further application in $^{15}$N NMR spectroscopy, metabolomics and spin hyperpolarisation experiments.
